exercises.title: Why pay full price for a prefix the provider has seen
exercises.prompt: Explain the problem prompt caching solves and how it works at the provider level. Using the lesson's coding-agent example, show the cost problem and how caching cuts it. What are the savings on cost and latency?
exercises.hint: You resend the same system prompt/context every turn and pay for it each time. Prompt caching keeps the prefix warm provider-side and bills ~10% on reuse. Example: 15k-token system prompt × 20 turns × 10k convos = $9k/day. Caching: 50-90% cost, 40-85% latency cut.
exercises.solution:
**The problem:** you send a large **unchanging prefix** with every request — a 4,000-token system prompt, 20,000 tokens of RAG context, tool definitions — and you **pay full input price for it every single time**, even though it never changes. The model needs it on every turn, and you can't shrink it without hurting quality, so naively you're billed repeatedly for identical text.

**The lesson's coding-agent example:** a coding agent sends the **same 15,000-token system prompt** to Claude on **every turn**. At $3/M input tokens, **20 turns** of just that prefix = `20 × 15,000 × $3/1e6 = $0.90` per conversation — **before the user's actual messages**. Multiply by **10,000 daily conversations** → **$9,000/day** for text that never changes. That's a brutal, pure-waste cost.

**How prompt caching works (provider level):** the provider **keeps the processed prefix 'warm' on their side** (the KV-cache / computed state for those tokens, Phase 10.12) and, on a subsequent request with the **same prefix**, **reuses it instead of recomputing** — billing you only a **fraction (~10%) of the normal input rate** for the cached portion. You mark the cacheable prefix (Anthropic `cache_control`), or the provider does it automatically (OpenAI), or you create explicit cached content (Gemini `CachedContent`). The prefix must be **byte-identical and contiguous** for a hit.

**The savings:**
- **Cost:** reused cached tokens bill at ~**10%** of normal, so for prompts dominated by a stable prefix you cut input cost by **50-90%**. The $9k/day example drops toward ~$1k-2k/day.
- **Latency:** because the provider **skips recomputing the cached prefix** (no re-running prefill over those tokens, Phase 10.12), **time-to-first-token drops 40-85%** — the model starts generating much sooner since it only processes the *new* tokens.
**Why it's free quality:** unlike compression or routing, prompt caching **changes nothing about the output** — same prompt, same model, same result — you just **stop paying full price for a prefix the provider already processed**. It's pure cost/latency win for any workload with a stable prefix (system prompts, tools, long shared context, multi-turn conversations), which is why all three major providers ship it as a first-class feature.

exercises.title: Cache-friendly prompt structure
exercises.prompt: Implement cache-friendly prompt layout and explain the rules: stable prefix first, dynamic content last, cache breakpoints, and the TTL/minimum-token constraints. Why does putting ANY dynamic content early break the cache for everything after it?
exercises.hint: Order: [system prompt][tools][stable context] = cacheable prefix, then [retrieved docs][history][user query] = dynamic. Cache works on exact prefix match; one changed early token invalidates the rest. Mind min token thresholds and short TTLs (5 min, or 1hr extended).
exercises.solution:
**Cache-friendly layout:**
```
[ system prompt        ]   ┐
[ tool definitions      ]   ├─ STABLE PREFIX (cacheable, mark breakpoint here)
[ fixed few-shot/context]   ┘
[ retrieved RAG docs    ]   ┐
[ conversation history  ]   ├─ DYNAMIC (changes per request, not cached / cached separately)
[ user query            ]   ┘
```
Put the **unchanging content first** (system prompt, tools, stable few-shot examples) as a contiguous **cacheable prefix**, mark a **cache breakpoint** at its end, and place all **variable content last** (retrieved docs, history, the query).

**The rules:**
- **Stable prefix first:** caching works on an **exact prefix match** — the cache stores the computed state up to a point, so everything you want cached must come **before** anything that changes.
- **Dynamic content last:** retrieved docs, history, and the query differ every request, so they go **after** the cached prefix (they'll be processed fresh, or cached at a later breakpoint if they have their own stable sub-prefix, like a long document reused across turns).
- **Cache breakpoints:** providers let you **mark where the cacheable prefix ends** (Anthropic's `cache_control`); the provider caches up to that point. You can have multiple breakpoints for nested stable segments.
- **TTL constraints:** caches are **short-lived** — typically **~5 minutes** of inactivity (Anthropic added a **1-hour extended TTL** option in 2025). If the prefix isn't reused within the TTL, it's evicted and the next request pays full price (a cache miss) plus a small **cache-write cost**. So caching pays off for **frequently-reused** prefixes (active conversations, high-traffic system prompts), not rare ones.
- **Minimum token thresholds:** there's usually a **minimum cacheable prefix size** (e.g. ~1,024 tokens) — caching tiny prefixes isn't supported/worthwhile.
- **Cache-write cost:** the *first* request (cache write) may cost slightly **more** than normal; you recoup it on reuse — so caching helps when reuse is likely.

**Why ANY dynamic content early breaks the cache for everything after it:** the cache is a **prefix cache** — it matches and reuses the computed state **only up to the first point where the input differs**. Because each token's representation depends on **all preceding tokens** (causal attention, Phase 7), changing **one early token invalidates the cached state for every token after it** — the provider must recompute from the first difference onward. So if you put the **user's query (which changes every request) at the top**, the prefix differs immediately and **nothing downstream can be cached** — you've destroyed the benefit. The entire technique hinges on keeping the **stable bytes contiguous at the front**; this is why cache-friendly layout is a direct extension of context engineering (Lesson 5): structure the window so the unchanging part is a clean, contiguous, front-loaded prefix.

exercises.title: Combining caching layers, provider differences, and when caching fails
exercises.prompt: Explain how provider-layer prompt caching combines with application-layer caching (Lesson 11) for 50-95% cost reduction, the differences across providers (Anthropic explicit cache_control, OpenAI automatic, Gemini CachedContent), the tradeoffs (TTL, write cost, cache misses), and when prompt caching doesn't help or backfires.
exercises.hint: App-layer: skip the call (exact/semantic cache). Provider-layer: cheapen the call's prefix. Stack them. Anthropic: manual breakpoints + TTL options; OpenAI: automatic; Gemini: explicit cached content objects. Fails when prefixes change every request, low reuse within TTL, or prefix too small.
exercises.solution:
**Combining app-layer and provider-layer caching (they stack):**
- **Application-layer caching (Lesson 11):** **avoid the API call entirely** — exact-match and semantic caches serve repeated/similar queries from your own cache (free, instant).
- **Provider-layer prompt caching (this lesson):** for the calls you **do** make, **cheapen the stable prefix** by ~90%.
They attack **different parts of the cost**: app-layer eliminates redundant *calls*; provider-layer reduces the cost of *each* call's prefix. A request that misses your app cache still benefits from prompt caching on its system prompt; a request that hits the app cache costs nothing. **Stacked** — app cache + model routing (Lesson 11) + prompt caching — you reach the **50-95% total cost reduction** the lessons cite. Both are expected in every serious 2026 production stack.

**Provider differences:**
- **Anthropic (Claude):** **explicit** — you mark cacheable segments with `cache_control` breakpoints, giving **fine-grained control** over what's cached. Default **~5-minute TTL**, with a **1-hour extended-TTL** option (2025). You manage where the prefix ends.
- **OpenAI:** **automatic** — the provider detects and caches repeated prefixes (over a min length) with **no code changes**; you just structure prompts with stable content first to benefit. Less control, less effort.
- **Google Gemini:** **explicit context caching** — you create a **`CachedContent` object** (e.g. a large document/context) with its own TTL, then reference it across requests. Good for **large reused context** (a big document queried many times).
All three: stable prefix first, mind TTL and minimum size; the difference is **manual control (Anthropic/Gemini) vs automatic (OpenAI)**.

**Tradeoffs:**
- **TTL:** short (5 min) caches only help **frequently-reused** prefixes; reuse must happen within the window or it's evicted (a miss). Extended TTL (1 hr) helps less-frequent reuse but may cost more to maintain.
- **Cache-write cost:** the first (write) request can cost **more** than a normal call; you only come out ahead if the prefix is **reused enough** to amortize it.
- **Cache misses:** if the prefix changes (even slightly) or isn't reused in time, you pay **full price + the write overhead** — worse than not caching.
- **Complexity:** explicit caching adds code/management; getting breakpoints wrong yields no benefit.

**When prompt caching doesn't help or backfires:**
- **Prefix changes every request:** if there's **no stable prefix** (every request has unique content up front, or dynamic content is placed early, previous exercise), there's nothing to cache — caching does nothing.
- **Low reuse within the TTL:** a prefix used **once** (or rarely, beyond the TTL) **never gets a cache hit**, so you pay the **write overhead for no payoff** — net *more* expensive. Caching needs **repetition within the TTL**.
- **Prefix below the minimum token threshold:** too-small prefixes aren't cacheable.
- **Highly personalized/varying system prompts:** if each user/request has a **different** system prompt, prefixes don't match and caching fails — restructure so the **shared** part is the cacheable prefix and the per-user part comes after.
- **Already-cheap, low-volume workloads:** the engineering effort isn't worth it.
**The decision rule:** prompt caching is a **near-free win when you have a large, stable, frequently-reused prefix** (system prompts, tools, shared long context, active multi-turn conversations) — structure the prompt cache-friendly (stable first), pick the provider's mechanism, and **stack it with app-layer caching and routing**. It **doesn't help** when prefixes are unique-per-request or reuse is too rare to beat the write cost — in those cases, focus on app-layer caching, routing, and compression (Lesson 11) instead. Measure your actual cache-hit rate; an unhit cache is just overhead.
